text: I’ll inspect the repo structure and available dependency tooling first, then use the bomly tools to identify exact vulnerable packages before editing manifests and lockfiles.

The runtime-filtered root scan did not discover the fixture subprojects, so I’m scanning each application directory directly without narrowing the scope first.

The scans show three patterns: direct upgrades for most packages, a Python `ecdsa` finding with no fixed version, and at least one Java `jackson-databind` finding marked not-fixed. I’m pulling the manifest files and targeted fix context next so the edits stay minimal.

For the webapp, `request` is deprecated and its vulnerable transitive chain has no patched `request` release according to bomly. I’m checking whether the app actually imports it; if it is unused, removing it is the least invasive remediation.

`request` is actively used in `src/importer.js`, so I’ll replace that single call with the already-present `axios` dependency. That removes the unfixable `request` package and its vulnerable transitives without changing the importer’s public behavior.

The Python changes are similarly narrow: upgrade PyJWT to the safe line and adapt the one encode return-type assumption. I’m also checking the intentionally pinned `urllib3` note before editing, because the initial enriched scan did not flag it despite the fixture comment.

I have enough context to edit. The version changes are: webapp direct upgrades plus `request` removal, Python PyJWT/urllib3 upgrades with `ecdsa` documented as no fixed version, and Java direct dependency upgrades while documenting the remaining not-fixed Jackson advisory.
I noticed the requirements input now has both the old and new PyJWT pins, so I’m cleaning that up before regenerating pinned dependency files.
Now I’m letting each package manager update its own lock/pin files, starting with npm so the dependency tree reflects the `request` removal and direct version upgrades.

tool: /bin/bash -lc 'npm install axios@1.16.0 express@4.20.0 jsonwebtoken@9.0.0 lodash@4.18.0 --save-exact && npm uninstall request'

text: npm completed, but it warned that `lodash@4.18.0` is a bad release despite bomly marking it as the fixed version. I’ll verify with bomly after the other edits; if the scanner still prefers 4.18.0, I’ll document that tradeoff rather than hiding it.
`pip-tools` is not installed in this environment, so I’m updating the pinned requirements file directly for the two pinned remediations instead of introducing tooling into the repo.
The manifests are edited. I’m going to run the per-app tests now, using online Maven once first if needed so the root offline Java test can see the upgraded dependencies.

text: All three per-app test suites passed after the edits. I’m rescanning with bomly now to confirm what remains, especially the advisories marked not-fixed.

The rescan found additional transitive webapp issues after the first upgrade: `cookie`, `qs`, and `send` still sit below their fixed versions through Express. I’m moving Express within the same major line to a version that carries those patched transitives.
bomly confirms those are Express transitives. I’m checking the available Express 4.x versions, then I’ll choose the smallest 4.x update that pulls patched `cookie`, `qs`, and `send`.
Express `4.22.2` is the first 4.x line with patched `cookie` and a `qs` range that can resolve above the scanner’s fixed version, so I’m updating to that and rescanning.
npm’s own audit is now clean for the webapp. I’m doing one more bomly scan for the webapp because that is the source of record for this fixture.
The webapp bomly scan is clean now. I’m adding `FIXES.md` with one entry per vulnerable package and then I’ll run the root `make test` to verify the full fixture workflow.

`FIXES.md` is in place. I’m running the root `make test` now, which exercises clean npm install, Python install, and offline Maven test as requested.
Root `make test` passed. I’m doing a final diff/status pass and cleaning any stale generated-comment metadata in the pinned files before wrapping up.

The remaining untracked instruction files were already present in the working tree; I’m leaving them alone. I’m making one small metadata correction in `requirements.txt` so its “via” comments match PyJWT’s new dependency.

- Java `jackson-databind` GHSA-5jmj-h7xm-6q6v / CVE-2026-54515: bomly reports `fix_state: not-fixed`.
